entry.title: Maintaining SAP EAM Master Data in SAP MDG Solution
entry.content:
SAP Master Data Governance (henceforth, it will be called as MDG in this blog) provides a central place to maintain master data used in procurement, maintenance & other processes and provides consistent data throughout the enterprise. It enables automatic distribution of master data w.r.t enterprise asset management (henceforth, it will called as EAM in this blog) application by system integration & approval processes.

Master data in MDG acts as a single source of truth which is created by multiple validations & approvals to meet the standards.

SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) supports only BuPA (Business Partners – customer & vendor) finance & material masters (as of now). Customers can get EAM masters like equipments, functional location & others through SAP partner solution like “utopia”. Refer to following links for more info:

SAP Help

SAP Website

EAM extension by Utopia for SAP Master Data Governance lets you create and control master data and enable effective asset lifecycle management and maintenance.

In this blog, I shall show how material bill of material (henceforth, it’s called MBOM) or Equipment bill of material (henceforth, it’s called EBOM) (building blocks of equipments) are managed in MDG solution/system & how it’s used in EAM application in SAP Fiori screen. This blog doesn’t focus on MDG architecture & its deployment options (like embedded or side-by-side).

MBOM can be created in MDG system through two or three steps/approvals as standard. Further approvals can be added through enhancements along with additional validations.

In this blog, I shall show the end-to-end scenario with the three steps/roles:

1) End user: Identifies, suggests & creates the new master data - BOM in SAP (MDG) system.

2) Lead: 1st level approver. Reviews & validates the master data. Maintains further data if required. This role/user is not available in the 2 step process.

3) MDG expert: Final approver & activates the BOM master data in SAP (MDG) system.

 

PFCG Roles Assignment:

SAP_BR_PRODMASTER_SPECIALIST

SAP_BR_PRODMASTER_STEWARD

 

MDG Configurations:

Kindly note about the transaction: MDGIMG. This is used to configure the MDG application including workflows, user or decision table steps & for many other activities.

Working Model:

Login as end user or the person who creates the master data in the system.

Open the section: “master data eam requestor” & the app “request new MBOM.” Choose two or three step process for master data creation.

Kindly note that you can see the apps for equipment & functional location creation. These are available only after the activation of Utopia add-on.

Fill all the mandatory fields & submit to get success message with the CR number (change request).

Kindly note that:

  • SAP provides few standard approval workflows & validations. It’s possible to add further custom fields, set default values & add validations through enhancements.  
  • On MBOM submit, sometimes the error like “MROBOM for material XXX plant YYYY is locked by change request". Earlier created MBOM with the same material & plant needs to be cleared in the Pending Change Request (either by approving or rejecting).   

Since the master data request or creation is done, let’s login as the specialist for validations, further data enhancements & approve/reject the CR.

Created MBOM request is available in “my inbox” app from where you can do the further analysis, changes & take action.

The MBOM detailed view opens in the new screen. Click on “edit” to change, add the item level material data & its details.

You can click on “display BOM hierarchy” button in the header data section to see the MBOM structure in the graphical or hierarchical view. If all data looks good, click on “finalize processing”.  This step will close the expected activities from the specialist.

Let’s login as the master data expert for the final approval process to activate & replicate the created master data in the SAP system. View the created master data MBOM request in the app “Approve EAM Master Data Request”.

Review all the data & click on “approve” or “reject” to close the master data creation activity.

Above created MBOM master data can be checked or viewed in the backend using the TA: CS03.

Created MBOM can be used in the equipment maintenance activity by adding it in the component field of the orderàoperations. All parameters of material are configured only once centrally & can be used it across the applications easily.  

Conclusion:

Create master data using the MDG to avoid any kind of duplications or discrepancies. Most of the customers are struggling to make the master data correct, lean & accurate from all the dimensions. MDG supports the entire lifecycle of the master data management with governance, validations, workflows & many other features.

entry.title: Integration of DMS and PM for Document Attachment
entry.content:
Introduction

Integrating SAP DMS (Document Management System) with PM (Plant Maintenance) helps companies easily manage and connect important documents to their maintenance tasks, making it simpler for maintenance teams to find the right information, follow rules, and keep equipment running smoothly.

Key Benefits of Integration

  • Improved Access to Documentation: Maintenance personnel can access relevant documents such as technical manuals, safety instructions, and equipment drawings directly from the maintenance work order, reducing time spent searching for information.
  • Enhanced Compliance and Quality: Integration ensures that the latest versions of documents are linked to maintenance orders, helping organizations meet regulatory requirements and maintain high-quality standards in maintenance operations.
  • Streamlined Processes: By linking documents directly to equipment, functional locations, and maintenance orders, the integration streamlines the process of retrieving necessary documentation, thereby reducing delays and improving maintenance efficiency.

Integration Points

  • Equipment and Functional Location Master Data: Documents can be linked to equipment and functional locations in SAP PM. This ensures that all relevant documentation (e.g., technical specifications, operation manuals) is accessible directly from the equipment or location master data.
  • Maintenance Orders and Notifications: Documents related to maintenance tasks, such as maintenance procedures, checklists, and safety instructions, can be attached to maintenance orders and notifications, providing technicians with all necessary information.
  • Work Centers: Documents can also be linked to work centers, ensuring that the personnel working at specific work centers have access to relevant documents.

Technical Aspects of Integration

  • Document Linking: Documents in SAP DMS are linked to PM objects (e.g., equipment, functional locations, maintenance orders) using object links. These links ensure that the documents are readily accessible from the relevant SAP PM transactions.
  • Document Types and Categories: SAP DMS allows categorizing documents into different types (e.g., technical drawings, maintenance manuals). These document types are defined in the DMS and linked to PM objects using object links.
  • Version Control: SAP DMS provides version control, ensuring that the most recent version of a document is available to maintenance personnel. When a document is updated, the new version is automatically linked to the relevant PM objects.
  • Security and Access Control: Security settings in SAP DMS ensure that only authorized users can access sensitive documents. Access control can be defined at the document level to restrict viewing, editing, or printing.

In this blog, let’s discuss how to attach documents to maintenance orders and maintenance notifications.

Steps for Integration Setup (configuration)

1.Define Document Types in SAP DMS:

Set up document types and categories in SAP DMS that will be used for linking with PM objects.

Path: Spro->IMG ->Cross application components -> Document management ->control data ->Define DMS document type ->New entries

Or T. code : DC10

2.Assign Document Types to PM Objects:

Assign the relevant document types to the PM objects. This configuration enables the display of documents in PM transactions.

Path: Spro ->IMG ->Cross application components -> Document management - >control data ->Define DMS document type -> select Document type -> click Define object link

Or T. code : DC10

Similarly, the required object needs to be linked to the document type

3.Assign DMS link Screen to PM Notification:

SPRO -> IMG -> Plant maintenance and customer Service -> Maintenance and service Processing -> maintenance and service notification -> Overview of Notification type ->Select Notification type ->click Screen Structure for Extended Views ->New entries

This setup allows users to conveniently access documents related to their maintenance notifications, streamlining the maintenance process.

Process

Step 1: Create Document, Attach Files, and Link to Object

T. Code: CV01N

Use the 'Object Links' tab to link the document to the relevant objects like equipment, functional locations, Notification and maintenance orders. This ensures that the document is easily accessible whenever the linked object is viewed or processed.

By completing these steps, you effectively integrate the document into SAP, making it available for maintenance and other relevant processes.

Step 2: Display Attached Documents in Maintenance Notifications and Orders

Once documents are attached and linked to maintenance objects, you can easily view them directly from maintenance notifications and orders. Here's how to access and display the attached documents.

1.Notification

2.Order

The attached documents will be displayed under the Additional Data tab in the order

Common Use Cases

  • Preventive Maintenance: Attach maintenance checklists and procedures to preventive maintenance orders to ensure that technicians follow the correct steps.
  • Breakdown Maintenance: Provide troubleshooting guides and technical manuals directly linked to maintenance notifications, aiding in faster resolution of breakdowns.
  • Safety and Compliance: Link safety instructions and compliance documents to equipment or maintenance orders to ensure adherence to safety standards.

By integrating SAP DMS with SAP PM, organizations can significantly enhance their maintenance processes, improve access to critical documentation, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. The seamless integration helps in achieving more efficient and effective plant maintenance operations.

entry.title: Why Failure-Mode Focus Matters in Intelligent Asset Management
entry.content:
In asset-heavy industries like Oil and Gas, Energy, or Transportation, managing equipment is more than just a cost factor—it's crucial for operations. Delivering gas under high pressure or transporting passengers at high speed requires a strong focus on asset reliability and, especially, asset integrity. This is the "license to operate." If a critical asset fails, it can lead to equipment damage, loss of production and revenue, and even more serious risks, like environmental harm or human injuries.

To avoid such problems, it's important to apply the right maintenance practices to critical assets. This helps manage risk in a complete way, improving the effectivity of measures and ensuring assets work their best.

Why Understanding Asset Failure is Critical

In industries that rely heavily on equipment, understanding how and why assets fail is essential. Maintenance planning and execution often depend on the experience of professionals, such as maintenance planners or technicians, who have been in their roles for many years. Some strategies follow recommendations from Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) or adhere to regulatory guidelines. However, there is often a significant gap between asset reliability engineering and maintenance planning and execution.

The Gap Between Maintenance Strategies and Execution

This gap appears when maintenance strategies—like managing failure data and recommendations—are handled separately by different teams. One group might focus on engineering, while another handle failure reporting and maintenance execution. This disconnection can cause inefficiencies. Similarly, proactive strategies such as classic preventive maintenance or even condition-based, predictive maintenance aren't always based on real evidence or fully integrated across teams.

To gain operational excellence and even enable new capabilities end-to-end, it's time to shift to a failure-mode centric approach in asset management.

How SAP Asset Performance Management Bridges the Gap

SAP Asset Performance Management (APM), integrated with Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA, addresses this opportunity by offering an end-to-end business process. From defining a maintenance strategy to execution, the system includes a feedback loop that enables continuous improvement, ensuring better maintenance over time.

 

The Failure Mode: A Common Language Between Teams

A key feature of this system is a unified and harmonized failure data model. As the central element, the failure mode acts as the common “language” between asset reliability engineering and maintenance teams. This unified approach prevents issues caused by inconsistent or misaligned data, allowing for better communication and coordination.

According to DIN EN 13306, a failure mode is the “manner in which the inability of an item to perform a required function occurs.” In simpler terms, it describes what goes wrong with an asset when it stops working as expected.

Industry Standards Inform SAP’s Failure Data Model

SAP’s failure data model draws from industry standards, such as FMEA (Failure Modes and Effects Analysis), FMECA (Failure Modes, Effects, and Criticality Analysis), and RCM (Reliability-Centered Maintenance). This model allows teams to define and prioritize maintenance strategies—whether reactive (fixing something after it breaks) or proactive (preventing it from breaking).

The model also considers other crucial details such as the operating environment, the asset’s function, the potential failure effect, the failure mechanism, and others. All these elements together provide a complete picture of possible failures and their consequences.

The Power of Relationships Between Failure Data

One of the most important aspects of this model is the relationships between the different elements. For example, the relationships between a failure mode and its addressed function and functional failure, its relevant failure effects, mechanisms, and potential causes are critical. It makes subsequent failure reporting and recording more efficient and targeted, especially when reacting to an issue.

Equally important is the connection between a failure mode and the recommended proactive measures (like preventive maintenance via maintenance plans). This connection ensures that maintenance strategies and failure reporting are linked, creating a cycle of continuous improvement. The practical lessons learned from maintenance feed back into the re-engineering, making future strategies more effective.

  

Figure 1 shows the unified failure data model with its entities, their relationships, and the intended usage in SAP Asset Performance Management as well as in Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA.

 

Failure Mode Accounting in Asset Management

Safeguarding the License to Operate

For businesses in asset-intensive industries, preventing catastrophic failures is critical to maintaining their “license to operate”. A major, or even catastrophic asset failure can lead to operational downtime, safety risks, and financial losses, which is why proactive maintenance strategies are essential.

A well understanding on asset criticality and failure consequence easily helps to properly rank the maintenance backlog and set the right focus.

The Unified Failure Data Model

With the unified failure data model, a “lingua franca”, or shared “language” is established between reliability engineers and maintenance planners or technicians. This model ensures efficient and consistent communication across different teams, helping improve both asset reliability and integrity. By defining critical failures and their consequences ahead of time, proactive measures—such as preventive or condition-based maintenance—can be implemented to prevent failures.

Closing the Loop: Failure Reporting and Feedback

When a failure does occur, the unified model supports a closed-loop reporting system. This failure-mode-centric approach in asset management can be compared to principles of financial accounting, where data allows for trend analysis across assets, helping teams identify areas for improvement and implement countermeasures as needed. The ongoing monitoring of the maintenance strategy ensures consistency, higher asset reliability and integrity.

 

A Comparison to Financial Accounting Principles

“The ultimate goal of any set of accounting principles is to ensure that a company’s financial statements are complete, consistent, and comparable.

This makes it easier for investors to analyze and extract useful information from the company’s financial statements, including trend data over a period of time. It also facilitates the comparison of financial information across different companies. Accounting principles also help mitigate accounting fraud by increasing transparency and allowing red flags to be identified.

Comparability is the ability for financial statement users to review multiple companies’ financials side by side with the guarantee that accounting principles have been followed to the same set of standards.

Accounting information is not absolute or concrete, and standards are developed to minimize the negative effects of inconsistent data. Without these rules, comparing financial statements among companies would be extremely difficult, even within the same industry. Inconsistencies and errors also would be harder to spot.”

 Source: Investopedia

 

 

Failure Mode Accounting: A Company Wide Strategy

This failure mode-centric approach forms the foundation for failure mode accounting throughout the entire company and beyond. By extending Enterprise Asset Management (EAM) capabilities provided by SAP S/4HANA with SAP Asset Performance Management (APM), businesses can seamlessly move to a more sophisticated level of asset management that integrates with supply chain, resource management, and finance.

 

Moving Towards Condition-Based Maintenance - Maintenance at the Right Time with the Right Scope

Challenges of Traditional Preventive Maintenance

Preventive maintenance, whether based on time or performance, is widely used to mitigate critical failures. However, this “one-size-fits-all” approach often falls short, leading to unexpected asset failures and breakdowns.

Adopting Condition-Based and Predictive Maintenance

The need for more advanced approaches like condition-based maintenance (CBM) or predictive maintenance is growing. SAP’s Asset Performance Management provides a holistic approach by combining real-time monitoring with historical failure data from SAP S/4HANA, enabling better asset health tracking and maintenance planning. If CBM is chosen as the maintenance strategy, the system integrates time-series data and interprets the asset’s remaining useful life. This, paired with failure modes and task recommendations, unlocks the full potential of CBM.

The Benefits of Integrated Asset Management

By fully integrating CBM into existing asset management processes, businesses can transition to a new era of asset management that targets optimal maintenance for maximum asset availability.

It’s the combination of all maintenance strategies – corrective, preventive, condition-based, predictive, prescriptive – in one unified system with a consistent and coherent management of failures and related data that allows a higher sophisticated maintenance program for a better business outcome.

 

SAP’s Intelligent Asset Management: 6+1 Key Steps - From Maintenance Strategy Definition to Execution, and Back

SAP supports six key business process steps to integrate Asset Performance Management and Enterprise Asset Management effectively. The foundational piece is the harmonized and aligned asset master data definition, which supports a closed-loop approach based on the Plan, Do, Check, Act (PDCA) principle:

0 - Manage Asset Master Data

Manage aligned and harmonized asset master data centrally for end-to-end processes in asset management spanning from reliability engineering to maintenance and inspection execution. E.g. manage:

  • core asset master data like equipment and technical objects
  • maintenance plans, task list and inspection plans, or maintenance rules and planning objects
  • measuring points and indicators to cover usage and condition

The inbuilt synchronization for the relevant asset master data between SAP Asset Performance Management and SAP S/4HANA keps both sides always on the latest. 

SAP Solution(s) in Focus: SAP Asset Performance Management, SAP S/4HANA (Asset Management)

1 - Define Asset Maintenance Strategy

Use standardized methods like Risk and Criticality Analysis (RCA), Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA), or Reliability Centered Maintenance (RCM) to define your maintenance strategy, resulting in:

  • Well-classified and segmented assets according to their criticality
  • A well-defined failure data model including failure modes for assets—either on an individual or asset class level, with the failure mode as the leading entity
  • Well-defined recommendations for reactive or proactive measures

SAP Solution in Focus: SAP Asset Performance Management

 2 - Define Maintenance Plans, Rules and Activities

Implement maintenance strategies via elements like

  • Task Lists / Inspection Plans for detail job or inspection characteristic specification; including linkage to addressed failure mode
  • Maintenance Plans for time- or performance-based schedule, and/or
  • Maintenance Rules for condition-based or predictive/prescriptive maintenance

With the connectivity between maintenance strategy (recommendation) and the implemented elements the traceability is made easy.

SAP Solution(s) in Focus: SAP Asset Performance Management, SAP S/4HANA (Asset Management)

 3 - Monitor Asset Health and Maintenance Demand

  • Report failures manually or automatically from sensor data to a fully prequalified maintenance request (notification) with reliable data
  • Use the prescribed failure data mode to classify the report appropriately and derive task list proposals from the system
  • Focus on critical work, build a consistent asset maintenance history for documentation, trending, and continuous improvement identification.

SAP Solutions in Focus: SAP Asset Performance Management, SAP S/4HANA (Asset Management)

4 - Plan Asset Maintenance Task and Resources

Plan maintenance or inspection jobs in detail with all needed resources in an efficient and effective way. Optimize the schedule for highest workforce utilization and distribute jobs to the rightly qualified technicians.

SAP Solution(s) in Focus: SAP S/4HANA (Asset Management), SAP S/4HHANA Asset Management for resource scheduling, SAP Field Service Management

5 - Perform Asset Maintenance

Execute maintenance or inspection jobs with all information at hand – either on the laptop or mobile device.

  • Document performed jobs with qualitative and quantitative information for proper financial settlement, material bookings and maintenance history building.
  • Loop back failure/malfunction or breakdown information based on the failure data model in a complete, consistent, and comparable manner.

SAP Solution(s) in Focus: SAP S/4HANA (Asset Management), SAP Service and Asset Manager, SAP Business Network for Asset Collaboration

6 - Analyze Asset and Maintenance Performance

Evaluate the efficiency and effectiveness of the defined, implemented, and executed maintenance and inspection program based on the unified and aligned failure data model.

  • Compare the initial “theoretical” definition of the maintenance program and failure data setup with the proven, evidence-based “practical” reality from the maintenance history
  • Take action—if needed and appropriate—to adjust the maintenance strategy for better business outcomes while mitigating risk

 SAP Solution(s) in Focus: SAP Asset Performance Management, SAP Analytics Cloud

  

Figure 2 illustrates the 6+1 key business process steps with listed business capabilities based on harmonized and aligned master data in the center for a closed-loop, intelligent asset management approach.

 

Conclusion: Revolutionizing Asset Management with SAP

 Seamless Integration of Asset Performance Management and Enterprise Asset Management

Seamlessly connecting Asset Performance Management (APM) and Enterprise Asset Management (EAM) revolutionizes asset management practices. This integration is crucial for asset-intensive industries but also benefits other sectors. The end-to-end (e2e) process—from defining asset maintenance strategies to performing maintenance and back—is essential for solving the complex equation of asset reliability, asset integrity, and maintenance cost.

Evidence-Based Maintenance for Operational Excellence

Ongoing, evidence-based monitoring of the optimal maintenance strategy for assets—whether critical with high consequences or not—enables asset managers to effectively mitigate risks and make informed decisions. This leads to enhanced operational excellence and better management of assets.

With SAP Asset Performance Management and Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA as the two light-house solutions in the overall SAP Intelligent Asset Management suite complemented by

  • SAP Service and Asset Manager
  • SAP S/4HANA Asset Management for resource scheduling
  • SAP Field Service Management
  • SAP Business Network for Asset Collaboration
  • SAP Integrated Business Planning for MRO

we believe in our strong contribution to unlock the full potential of complex assets and strive for higher business outcome.  We are dedicated to helping make the world’s assets operate effectively.

 

Elmar Paul

Chief Process Owner, Cloud ERP DSC Operate

entry.title: Announcing the first “Evento Internacional para la Distribución de Gas” Buenos Aires, Febraury 21-22
entry.content:
The first edition of the international event for gas distribution in Latin America seeks to share knowledge and experiences in the digital transformation of natural gas distribution companies, and to address practices and lessons learned in the adoption of new technologies, based on the exchange of information among peers and the exploration of successful use cases.

 

The meeting aims to highlight recent innovations in natural gas distribution technology by showcasing success stories in digital transformation.

Some highlights of the agenda:

  • Key note of welcome
  • Challenges of energy in Latin America in the international context
  • Operational Efficiency
  • Commercial Efficiency
  • Sustainability

Thanks to our sponsor IBM, the Executive Networking Dinner help attendees to join an executive community that extends beyond the meeting, gaining knowledge from  peers and other companies, partners and vendors.

For those interested to join and take part in our discussions we have set up a registration website where updates on agenda and other useful information is as well...

Looking forward to see you in Buenos Aires!

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2409
entry.content:
In our latest release, we’ve made significant improvements to help you work smarter and faster, for example, we've simplified and enhanced the user experience in both risk and criticality assessments and RCM assessments. We've also introduced some great new features in recommendation management improving traceability with comments for status changes. Whether you're in the field or the office, these updates increase efficiency and usability across the board. Read on to learn more about all the new features!

 

Asset Risk Assessment

Enhanced UX for Risk & Criticality Assessments: Simplified Scale Definitions

We’re excited to unveil significant UX improvements for the the risk and criticality assessment. Previously, accessing essential scale definitions required multiple clicks, which was particularly cumbersome for users on tablets. To enhance the experience, we’ve introduced a new table view that seamlessly displays long descriptions directly within the assessment screen. This update eliminates the need for additional clicks and ensures a smoother, more efficient workflow, especially for those using tablets in the field.

This update not only streamlines the process but also integrates seamlessly with existing features, including the Financial Risk field, Notes section, and the 10x10 matrix. Looking ahead, it also accommodates the future requirement for Leading Failure Modes.

With these enhancements, Reliability Engineers can now view all relevant information in one place, improving efficiency and ease of use. This change reflects our commitment to refining user experience and responding to feedback, ensuring a more intuitive and effective assessment process.

Watch the video:

Asset Reliability Engineering

Select Multiple Recommendations for Rework and View Comments with Status Changes

You can now select multiple recommendations and simultaneously create a comment for all, enhancing both efficiency and the ability to track the history of the recommendation. Rework can be done for recommendations with status:

  • Released

  • Implementation in Process

With this, the recommendation will be set to status In Process and sent back to the Manage Recommendations application for reliability engineer to reprocess the recommendation. You can view the most recent comments on the list page using the Comment icon. 

Watch the video:

For more information, see the SAP Help Portal Implement Recommendations.

Enhanced Recommendation Management: Commenting on Status Changes

Recording comments during status changes provides context and traceability in recommendation management. The Information tab has a Comments section to view the recorded comments.

You can record comments for recommendation with the following statuses:

  • In Process → On Hold

  • In Process → Rejected

  • On Hold → In Process

  • For Review → In process

  • For Review → On Hold

  • For Review → Rejected

  • Ready for Release → In Process

  • Ready for Release → On Hold

  • Ready for Release → Released

You can also view the most recent comments by choosing the Comment icon on the list view page. 

Watch the video to see it in action:

Support Multiple Technical Objects in RCM Assessments

You can now select and assess multiple technical objects simultaneously as long as they share the same class, failure data profile, and planning plant details. For more information, see Assigning Technical Objects.

Watch the video: 

Improved UX in RCM Assessment - New Three Panel Layout

A three panel layout is used to display the failure data and associated evaluations. The Create or Edit Consequence Assessment buttons allow you to view the consequence evaluation using the third panel. On the Assessment page, there is an option to enter full screen mode. Watch the video to find out more:

APIS for FMEA Assessments

You can use public APIS to create, update, read, and delete FMEA assessments. For more information, see API Services.

 

Keep In Touch

Here's a quick reminder how to keep in touch with all things APM:

  • Check out all past and present Intelligent Asset Management webcasts here. If you register for an IAM webcast, you will be automatically subscribed to our IAM Community Engagement Newsletter. 
  • Check some of our recent blogs:
    • Why Failure-Mode Focus Matters in Intelligent Asset Management 
    • What's New SAP Asset Performance Management 2408
    • What's New SAP Asset Performance Management 2407 
    • What's New SAP Asset Performance Management 2406 
    • What's New SAP Asset Performance Management 2405 
    • What's New SAP Asset Performance Management 2404
    • SAP Asset Performance Management Embedding Cumulocity IoT to Drive Innovations in IoT and AI
    • How AI-based Anomaly Detection empowers reliability engineers in SAP Asset Performance Management
    • SAP Asset Performance Management – FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
    • What's New SAP Asset Performance Management 2403
  • Subscribe to What's New on the SAP Help Portal
  • Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session

See you again soon for the 2410 release update!

entry.title: Preventive Maintenance assistance for SAP PM and SAP EAM using IBM watsonx
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's fast-paced production industry, unscheduled downtime and equipment breakdowns can cause major operational interruptions and financial losses. To overcome this, businesses are increasingly resorting to proactive techniques that use cutting-edge technology to efficiently manage their assets. One such method is to integrate AI-driven solutions into current corporate systems.

 This article explores how combining Watsonx.ai with SAP's Plant Maintenance (PM) Module and Asset Management Platform can help create a virtual assistant for preventive maintenance. The technology aims to automate asset monitoring, identify potential breakdowns, and schedule timely interventions, ensuring continuous production while reducing unexpected maintenance costs.

 

Architecture

In this architecture, we have SAP S/4HANA at the core, which integrates SAP Plant Maintenance (PM) and SAP Enterprise Asset Management (EAM). These modules handle the maintenance and asset lifecycle management processes within the system. The data exchange between SAP S/4HANA and the front-end interface happens through SAP OData services, which efficiently transfer data to and from a SAP UI5 application—in this case, a Preventive Maintenance Application.

This SAP UI5 Preventive Maintenance Application is enhanced with a conversational layer powered by Watsonx Assistant, allowing users to interactively query and retrieve real-time details about plant operations and asset conditions. Watsonx Assistant is built upon Watsonx.ai, an AI platform capable of processing both structured and unstructured data to provide insights and recommendations related to the maintenance and management of critical assets.

Additionally, SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) serves as a critical integration hub in this architecture. It connects various SAP services and applications, enabling seamless data flow between systems. SAP BTP also incorporates SAP Gen AI Hub service, which can extend the capabilities of the system by integrating with external AI platforms, such as IBM Cloud, which hosts watsonx.ai.

One of the key features of this architecture is its versatility in data sourcing. While SAP HANA serves as the primary database for SAP applications, watsonx assistant can also interface with non-SAP databases, enabling the system to collect, analyse, and utilize data from a variety of sources. This opens the door for more comprehensive insights by merging data from disparate systems, enhancing the overall predictive maintenance capabilities of the virtual assistant. 

 

 Value Proposition 

  • Comprehensive Maintenance Schedules 
    • Time-based Maintenance: Ensures that equipment is maintained on a regular basis at predetermined intervals, lowering the chance of unexpected malfunctions. These schedules may be automated using Watsonx Assistant, which improves adherence to maintenance timeframes while eliminating manual involvement. 
    • Performance-based maintenance: It helps monitors asset performance parameters and initiates maintenance actions when equipment performance begins to depart from ideal circumstances. Integration with WatsonX Assistant allows for predictive insights and faster response to performance deterioration. 
    • Condition-based repair: Monitors real-time equipment conditions using IoT data, allowing for repair when certain thresholds or abnormalities are discovered. The Watsonx assistant may use this information to automate maintenance triggers and maximise asset uptime.  

  • Seamless IoT Integration for Automated Inspections
    • By interacting with an IoT Hub, Watsonx Assistant automates inspection operations by gathering real-time data from equipment sensors. This eliminates manual inspections, decreases the possibility of human mistake, and assures that equipment always operates within set safety and operational limitations.
    • Automated warnings and suggestions based on IoT data can motivate preventive maintenance choices, hence increasing overall operational efficiency.  

  • Streamlined SAP Workflow Management: 
    • Watsonx Assistant improves the administration of important SAP activities including generating, maintaining, and presenting equipment and functional locations. It streamlines the processing of asset information and maintenance records, minimising administrative bottlenecks. 
    • Simplifies the process of maintaining maintenance task lists, allowing plant managers to easily prioritise, allocate, and carry out maintenance tasks. 
    • Allows for the approval and release of maintenance orders via a conversational interface, which speeds up decision-making and reduces delays caused by human participation. 

  • Boosting Operational Efficiency: 
    • Automating procedures and using intelligent maintenance schedules enhances asset availability, uptime, and lowers operating costs. 
    • Watsonx Assistant's integration with SAP enables seamless communication across teams, enhancing collaboration and real-time decision-making.    

Business Outcome:  

  • Increased Operational Efficiency: With the integration of watsonx assistant into SAP’s preventive maintenance, businesses can automate maintenance scheduling and task execution. This reduces the manual workload, optimizes resource allocation, and streamlines maintenance operations. By ensuring equipment is serviced based on real-time data and performance metrics, operations run smoothly without unnecessary interruptions, boosting overall productivity. 
  • Reduced Downtime Costs: By leveraging real-time insights from IoT sensors and predictive maintenance capabilities, watsonx assistant helps prevent unplanned equipment failures. This proactive approach minimizes unexpected downtime, ensuring continuous production and reducing the significant costs associated with halting operations for emergency repairs.  
  • Extended Equipment Lifespan: Regular, condition-based, and performance-based maintenance prolongs the life of critical machinery. By addressing wear and tear before it escalates into serious damage, businesses can avoid costly replacements or overhauls, maximizing the return on investment in their equipment assets. 
  • Improved Safety and Compliance: Automating inspections and maintenance ensures that equipment consistently operates within safety standards and regulatory requirements. watsonx assistant can help ensure that safety protocols are followed, alerting teams when maintenance is overdue or when equipment is performing outside acceptable parameters, reducing the risk of accidents or compliance violations. 
  • Enhanced Customer Satisfaction: Consistent operational efficiency and reduced downtime lead to fewer disruptions in service or product delivery. watsonx assistant’s role in optimizing maintenance ensures that customers receive reliable, timely products and services, leading to higher customer satisfaction and stronger business relationships.

Business Features 

  • Enhanced and Quick Assistance for Asset Troubleshooting: watsonx assistant provides real-time troubleshooting support for operators and maintenance teams. By leveraging AI-driven insights, it quickly identifies potential asset issues, suggests solutions, and provides step-by-step guidance. This reduces troubleshooting time and helps resolve problems before they escalate, minimizing the impact on production. 
  • Automated Analysis of Inspection and Maintenance Activity with Detailed Report Generation: The assistant automates the analysis of inspection data and maintenance logs, generating comprehensive reports without manual input. This feature ensures that maintenance activities are tracked, and detailed summaries of inspections are created, giving managers clear insights into equipment health, compliance, and performance.   
  • User-Friendly Conversational Flow for Operator and Supervisor Persona: With an intuitive conversational interface, watsonx assistant tailors interactions for different user personas like operators and supervisors. It simplifies complex maintenance tasks, offering guidance in an easy-to-understand language, making the entire process more efficient and accessible for all users involved in equipment management. 
  •  Limitless Data Insights and Exploration Capability: watsonx assistant provides powerful analytics and exploration tools for maintenance data. Users can dive deep into equipment performance, historical maintenance records, and predictive insights, enabling informed decision-making and the discovery of hidden patterns or trends that can enhance preventive maintenance strategies. 
  • Ability to Understand Natural Language Queries and Retrieve Precise Answers: Equipped with natural language processing (NLP), watsonx assistant can understand user queries in everyday language. Whether an operator asks for specific equipment information, upcoming maintenance tasks, or troubleshooting advice, the assistant delivers precise answers instantly, reducing the need to sift through complex data manually.  

Conclusion 
Integrating Watsonx Assistant into preventive maintenance operations introduces an AI-driven approach to asset management. It enhances troubleshooting, automates analysis, and provides comprehensive data, helping businesses maintain operational efficiency and minimize downtime. The conversational interface supports both operators and supervisors, streamlining maintenance processes and integrating with SAP systems. Watsonx Assistant enables teams to make informed decisions by analysing maintenance data using natural language processing and data insights. This integration aims to extend equipment lifespan, improve safety, and enhance compliance within maintenance activities, making it a practical addition to asset management strategies. 

Credits: 
Dhara Bagadia 
Tushar Trivedi

entry.title: Configuring Failure Curve Analytics (FCA) In SAP Asset Performance Management (APM)
entry.content:
Have you ever wondered how to predict the Probability Of Failure (PoF) for a technical object associated with a specific failure mode based on its age?. In this blog, I would like to highlight the essential features of Failure Curve Analytics (FCA) in SAP Asset Performance Management (APM).

Failure Curve Analytics (FCA) is a feature within APM that allows the maintenance team to predict when a technical object could fail and how likely  failure would occur given the age of the technical object. The graph that FCA produces is based on Weibull model, a commonly used data model within the realm of reliability. System uses malfunction notifications to train and score the FCA model and visualize the failure data. On the graph, we can view upper and lower confidence intervals (the range in values of likely failure given the technical object`s age) and Probability Of Failure (PoF).

SAP Asset Performance Management

My Business Use Case:

We run an air compressor with a standby equipment continuously to operate the kiln girth gear lubrication system. As a maintenance engineer, I would like to predict the Probability Of Failure (PoF) using Failure Curve Analytics (FCA) in SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) for one of the air compressors before the planned outage in Jul 2025.

My Technical Object Structure in S/4 HANA Asset Management:

MTTR/MTBR Analysis for the technical object 10001729 in S/4 HANA Asset Management:

Before we dive into Failure Curve Analytics, I would also like to touch base on other alternatives to predict next failure date for the technical object 10001729 using MTBF(Mean Time Between Failure) average values. 

  • I assume failure occurs at regular intervals and the next failure date is simply:

  • I assume failure occurs randomly over time and use random model with exponential distribution to predict next failure date:

 

Now, let us dive into Failure Curve Analytics and see when the Air Compressor will fail if it runs continuously.

First things first, check the integrity of the Air Compressor 10001729 in SAP APM.

To view the actual failure curve graph, we need to create a FCA model. FCA model needs notifications to train and score. For now, all the notifications & failure modes that will be used to train and score have already been created in the S/4 HANA Asset Management system that is associated with the SAP APM system. 

Create a Technical Object Group and assign the respective technical objects which are being used for FCA model.

 

 

Creating Failure Curve Analytics Model:

 

If we have any model configurations, we can see them once we click on "Configure Failure Curve Analytics".

I have created a new FCA model as,

  • The age for conditional probability of failure is used to calculate the conditional probability of the failure. For example, if you enter 100 days as the age and your technical object has a current age of 50 days, then the conditional probability of failure is calculated for the age of 100 days given that the technical object has not failed until the age of 50 days. If you leave blank, then system calculates only the probability of failure.
  • First Installation date is used to calculate the age of the technical object at its first failure. If installation date is not maintained, the first failure date is used to calculate the age of the technical object at its next failure.
  • If technical object is repairable and restored to a "like-new" condition and set to the age of 0 once it is repaired. This age is used to calculate the age of the technical object at its next failure. If not repairable, the installation date is used to calculate the age of the technical object at its failure.

Under Input Data Sets, let us assign the technical objects that are being trained and scored within the FCA model. We can "Add" or "Remove" the technical objects from the model.

Now, let us assign the failure modes. All the failure modes associated with the failure data profile of the technical object used in FCA model are displayed. We can check off the failure modes to be excluded from training and scoring of the model.

Note: It is considered to have no more than 40 failure modes associated with the model.

Now let us specify the malfunction notification date range to train and score the FCA model. A default date range is provided by the system, although this can be altered if desired.

 

Training and Scoring the Model:

With all the input Data Sets properly configured, the FCA model AC_SL200_FCA can now be trained and scored.

If the training and scoring fails, system displays the failure list that prevented the model from completing successful training and scoring and the status will be set to "Failed".

If the training and scoring of the model are both successful, the status of the both will be set to "Completed".

 

If we want to run the model at a different interval, we can still change the interval to DAY, WEEK & MONTH.

 

Viewing the Analytics Graph of the FCA Model:

Now the FCA Model "AC_SL200_FCA" has been successfully trained and scored. The FCA analytical graph can be viewed within the technical object details page. 

Let us navigate to "Explore Technical Objects" tile.

 

on the graph, there are three curves. The main one is probability of failure curve, which is the likely percent chance the equipment 10001729 will fail because of the failure mode "Low Discharge Pressure" selected at a specified age in days. The upper and lower confidence intervals mark the highest and lowest percent chance that the equipment 10001729 will fail due to the "Low Discharge Pressure" failure mode. The predicted failure date is Jan 8, 2025, and the time to failure is  47 days.

Key Note:

The MTBF approach to predict next failure date based on average values can mask variations and specific trends in the failure data. They assume constant failure rate, and might not reflect reality.

The FCA with Weibull distribution is more flexible and can represent various failure modes and can model different failure behaviors over time. When we consider systems and their components, they often do not fail at constant rate, So the FCA analysis can account for varying failure rates over time and fits the actual failure data to a curve and predicts when future failures might occur, where as the MTBF cannot. 

This brings me to the end of my process walk-through. I hope this blog proves useful to Plant Maintenance team. I look forward to seeing Plant Maintenance team using SAP APM Failure Curve Analytics to predict the technical object failure. 

If you would like to keep up on SAP Sustainability Control Tower, Asset Management and Asset Performance Management, follow me on SAP Community and LinkedIn.

 

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy.C

My LinkedIn Profile: www.linkedin.com/in/pardhreddyc

entry.title: Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition | 2023 FPS02 Release
entry.content:
Welcome back to my blog post series about Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition!

On the 8th October 2024, SAP has released the second “Feature Pack Stack (FPS)”, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition for the 2023 release.
For a comprehensive overview of selected innovation highlights across all lines of business, see the blog post 

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition | 2023 FPS02 Release – Part 1

In this blog post we will dive deeper into the innovations for Maintenance Management as part of the line of business Asset Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Release 2023 FPS02. 

 

Table of Contents

Enhancements in area of Phase Model 

  • Skipping screening phase in reactive maintenance process
  • Enhanced configuration for assigning overall status profile
  • Evaluation of maintenance order before entering next subphase

Inspection Checklist Process

  • Inspection Checklist Process Enhancements
  • Integrated checklist-handling in Find Technical Objects and Find Maintenance Orders and Operations
  • New SAP Fiori app Manage Inspection Checklists

Closed Loop

  • Integrated, cross-app navigation from the Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders app and Find Maintenance Orders app through BAdI implementation
  • Cross-application navigation from maintenance-order processing to permit to work 
  • Recommendations from SAP Asset Performance Management in the SAP Fiori app “Create Maintenance Request”
  • Enhanced integration of SAP Asset Performance Management into task lists and maintenance items for SAP Fiori apps
  • Providing a catalog-based “Failure Effect” field in the SAP Fiori apps for maintenance requests
  • Enabling situation handling for maintenance task lists and maintenance plans 

Enhancements for Perform Maintenance Jobs app

  • Overview of Team assignments
  • Overview of PRT assignments
  • Overview of planned Services
  • Planned Work and Remaining Work
  • Business Catalog for Cross-App Navigation

Purchase Contract Details in Maintenance Orders and Task Lists

Improve accuracy of material costs in refurbishment process

AI-assisted Maintenance Order Recommendation

Mass change of custom fields using the “Manage Maintenance Plans” and “Manage Maintenance Items” apps

Mass Generation of Permit to Work Objects

More Information / Additional Links

 

Enhancements in area of Phase Model 

Configuring Requirements for Creating Phase-Enabled Orders - Skipping screening phase in reactive maintenance process

If you create maintenance requests and maintenance orders of a specific type, these maintenance requests and orders are processed according to phases and become part of a comprehensive end-to-end process. In this business process, a standard logic controls the prerequisites that must be fulfilled before you can create a
phase-enabled maintenance order in the Planning phase. The underlying SAP standard logic is as follows:

  • Maintenance orders of the Reactive Maintenance order type must always be based on an accepted
    maintenance request. The application logic of the order evaluates that the header notification has been accepted during the screening phase, i.e. system status APOK (Approval: OK) has been activated. In other words: You can only create a reactive maintenance order in the Planning phase if the corresponding maintenance request has the system status APOK (Approval: OK) and is in the Accepted (Request) subphase. As soon as the accepted maintenance request has been assigned to a maintenance order, it is renamed to "maintenance notification". This header notification requires a priority and a final due date.
    In the case that the header notification does not include this active system status, the reactive maintenance order will not be created.
  • Maintenance orders of the Proactive Maintenance order type do not necessarily need to be based on a
    maintenance request. They can also be created based on a maintenance plan, for example. If you base a proactive maintenance order on a maintenance request, the maintenance request does not need to have run through an approval process.

With the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Release 2023 FPS02, configuration and a Business Add-In (BAdI) is provided that allows you to overwrite the standard application logic:

The new Business Add-In (BAdI) BAdI: Processing of Phase-Enabled Maintenance Orders
(EAM_CREATE_PHASE_ENABLED_ORDER) and the Customizing activity Define Requirements for Creating
Phase-Enabled Orders (Customizing view V_EAM_NOTIFCNFG) allow you to determine whether maintenance
requests need to be approved before phase-enabled maintenance orders can be based on them.

With the Customizing view V_EAM_NOTIFCNFG and the BAdI method CREATE_PHASE_ENABLED_ORDER, you can determine different combinations of planning plants, notification types and order types for which you want to implement a business logic different from the SAP standard described above. On the level of the maintenance planning plant, you can determine for each combination of notification type and order type whether an accepted maintenance request is mandatory for creating the corresponding phase-enabled maintenance order. For example, you can determine that in a specific planning plant, reactive maintenance orders can be created based on maintenance requests that have not been accepted. Or you can determine that proactive maintenance orders only can be created based on accepted maintenance requests.

In the example below,

  • a reactive maintenance order of type YA01 and maintenance planning plant 1010 can be raised against a notification of type M1. 
  • the notification Type M1 has no Approval Required flag active, so that any notification of that type will not enter the screening phase at all. Hence, those notification will never have system status APOK (Approval: OK) active.
  • without this configuration, the reactive maintenance order could not have been raised against a notification of type M1.

You can access the corresponding activity in Customizing: Plant Maintenance and Customer Service -> Maintenance and Service Processing -> Fiori Apps for Maintenance Processing -> General Settings -> Define Requirements for Creating Phase-Enabled Orders

Figure 1: Customizing - Change View “View for controlling the creation of phase-enabled orders”: Overview

In addition, see Customizing: Plant Maintenance and Customer Service -> Maintenance and Service Processing -> Maintenance and Service Notifications -> Overview of Notification Type

Figure 2: Customizing - Change View “Partner Functions, Approval”: Details

In the case that the configuration does not fulfil your business requirements, you can implement the BAdI EAM_CREATE_PHASE_ENABLED_ORDER. If you have entered the corresponding data in the Customizing view, the BAdI is called in the following cases:

  • You create a phase-enabled maintenance order in the Create Maintenance Order app.
  • You create a phase-enabled maintenance order in the Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders app.
  • You create a phase-enabled maintenance order via the BAPI BAPI_ALM_ORDER_MAINTAIN. 

(🔙Table of Contents)

 

Enhanced configuration for assigning overall status profile

If you process your maintenance orders according to phases, your business transactions lead to specific system statuses that are set on maintenance order or order operation level. These system statuses determine
the subphases and phases to which the corresponding maintenance order or order operation is passed. The subphases with their conditions and sequence throughout the maintenance process are bundled in an
overall status profile that is assigned to an order type. We deliver a standard configuration for the Reactive
Maintenance and Proactive Maintenance process.

Up to now, you could only specify the overall status profile depending on the order type.

Figure 3: Prior 2023 FPS02 release: Customizing view - Assign Overall Status Profile to Order Type

However, you might need to modify this standard configuration slightly depending on your various
maintenance planning plants. With the 2023 FPS02 release, you can also specify the valid overall status profiles depending on a combination of order type and maintenance planning plant.

Figure 4: Starting with 2023 FPS02 release: Customizing view - Assign Overall Status Profile to Order Type

You can access the corresponding activity in Customizing: Plant Maintenance and Customer Service -> Maintenance and Service Processing -> Fiori Apps for Maintenance Processing -> General Settings -> Configure Overall Status Profiles

Both screenshots shown above have the same consequence, all maintenance orders of type YA01 regardless of their maintenance planning plant will be assigned to the same overall status profile PMSP1.

Note:

  • Following restriction note 3365558, the configuration of the phase model is delivered by SAP and customers are not allowed to modify this configuration.
  • Future enhancements might remove some restrictions, so that customers might have several overall status profiles assigned to different combinations of maintenance planning plant and order types.

(🔙Table of Contents)

 

Evaluation of maintenance order before entering next subphase

A phase-model enabled maintenance order is assigned to an overall status profile which defines the conditions (using system statuses) determining each subphase of the order header and operation.

The new Business Add-In (BAdI) BAdI: Evaluation of Maintenance Order Data Including Buffer
(EAMS_EVAL_ORDER_DETAILS) allows you to evaluate data of phase-enabled maintenance orders each time
the maintenance orders are created or passed to a new subphase. This enables customers to introduce their custom logic to prevent the transition of the maintenance order into the next subphase.

As an example, a maintenance order has been released and the execution of this business transaction would determine a new subphase so that this order would be placed in the preparation phase.
Due to custom logic implemented in that BADI and the actual data of that maintenance order, the execution of this business transaction can be blocked so that this order remains in the current phase/subphase.

The BAdI reads maintenance order data from the buffer before the order data is saved on the database. You can evaluate this maintenance order data and implement warning or error messages that prevent the system from saving the order changes from the buffer to the data base. Note that you cannot use this BAdI to directly change any order data.
With this BAdI, you can evaluate and compare the following maintenance order data:

  • Order header data
  • Order operation data
  • Order operation component data
  • Data of the order object list
  • Phase control codes set on order header and on order operation level

This BAdI is called if you process phase-enabled maintenance orders

  • on the SAP Web User Interface for Plant Maintenance (PM)
  • in SAP Fiori apps
  • via the BAPI BAPI_ALM_ORDER_MAINTAIN
  • via the RAP-based API Maintenance Order (Version 2)

(🔙Table of Contents)

 

Inspection Checklist Process

For using the inspection checklist process

  • the business function LOG_EAM_CHECKLIST must be activated,
  • the checklist functionality must be enabled.

Check out also the Expert Talk: Inspection Checklist in Maintenance Management – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

We launched the inspection checklist solution first with the 2021 release and enhanced it with 2023 FPS01 release. What's new with FPS02?

Inspection Checklist Process Enhancements

In this chapter the description of each enhancement starts with a figure about the Phase Model for the Maintenance Process to provide you the context for phase-enabled orders. 

Maintenance Order: New Form Template for the Output Management

Figure 5: Phase Model for the Maintenance Process

With this feature, you can enhance a maintenance order by a new output type (CHECKLIST_REPORT for application object type MAINTENANCE_ORDER) and a new form and email template (EAM_MJP_CHECKLIST_REPORT / EAM_MJP_CL_DFLT_TMPL) . This enhancement is supported both by the classic SAP GUI as well as by WebDynpro.

Figure 6: 'Change Maintenance Order' app - new Output Type and Form Template

The new form template is an enhanced version of the old form with the following improvements:

  1. Barcode information is available for the checklist and is printed.
  2. Information on order and selected operations are printed.
  3. Checklist type of the operation is printed.
  4. Control indicator gets printed:
    + Required characteristic
    + Optional characteristic
    + After accepted characteristic
    + After rejected characteristic
    + Calculated characteristic (informational)
  5. Inspection characteristic and control indicator is printed together to avoid single lines
    at the end of a page

Figure 7: Maintenance Order: New Form Template for the Output Management

Note:

  • Deactivated and canceled checklists are not printed.
  • Inspection characteristics with the control indicators Do Not Print and No Charc Recording are not printed.
  • The fom template is translated.

 

Work Pack: New Form Template for Output Management

Figure 8: Phase Model for the Maintenance Process

With this feature, you can enhance the standard inspection checklist process for Work Pack Creation or Print in the Find Maintenance Order (F2175) and Find Maintenance Order and Operation (F2173) apps. With the new form and email template (PM_ORD_CHECKLIST_REPORT / EAM_MAINT_ORD_CL_DFLT_TMPL), the new output type (CHECKLIST_REPORT for application object type MAINTENANCE_JOB_PACKAGE), and the new grouping criterion (Operation Checklist Type), you can bundle job packs based on the checklist type functionality of order operations.

Figure 9: 'Find Maintenance Order' app - New grouping attribute 'Operation Checklist Type'

Figure 10: Work Pack - new Output Typ, Form Template, and Grouping Attribute

The new form template is an enhanced version of the old form with the following improvements (find the numbering also on the screenshot below):

  1. Barcode information is available for the checklist and is printed.
  2. Work pack/job pack information is printed.
  3. Checklist type of the operation is printed.
  4. Control indicator gets printed:
    + Required characteristic
    + Optional characteristic
    + After accepted characteristic
    + After rejected characteristic
    + Calculated characteristic (informational)
  5. Inspection characteristic and control indicator is printed together to avoid single lines
    at the end of a page

Figure 11: Work Pack / Job Pack - New Form Template for the Output Management

Note:

  • Deactivated and canceled checklists are not printed.
  • Inspection characteristics with the control indicators Do Not Print and No Charc Recording are not printed.
  • The fom template is translated.

 

Perform Maintenance Job app (F5104A)

Figure 12: Phase Model for the Maintenance Process

With the Perform Maintenance Job app you can now create follow-on notifications for inspection checklists by selecting the new button (see red dotted line in the screenshot below) in the checklists table of a job. The popover that is displayed when you select the status bar in the checklists table now shows additional details, including the usage decision valuation of the relevant inspection lot (see green dotted line). You can also display the usage decision valuation in the results table (see yellow dotted line) to inform the technician, if the inspection lot is still open for results recording.

Figure 13: 'Perform Maintenance Job' app enhancements

The Create Follow-On Notification uses the Notification Type from the Configure Checklists for Maintenance Order Types and Planning Plants activity and the technical object from the inspection lot.  A reference to the notification in the document flow is created.

Figure 14: 'Configure Checklists for Maintenance Order Types and Planning Plants' activity

Note: The Create Follow-On Notification is disabled, if the inspection lot is deactivated or has a recorded usage decision.

 

Final Time Confirmation: New system check of operations with checklists

New customizing flag Check Results Recording Status at Final Confirmation is available in customizing activity Configure Checklists for Maintenance Order Types and Planning Plants.

Figure 15: Customizing activity 'Configure Checklists for Maintenance Order Types and Planning Plants'

When the flag is set then the system checks if results have been recorded for all required or processed inspection characteristics. This check gets bypassed for deactivated, canceled or usage decision valuated inspection lots.

Final time confirmation check failed in IW41 transaction:

Figure 16: Final time confirmation check failed in IW41 transaction

Final time confirmation check failed in Perform Maintenance Jobs app:

Figure 17: Final time confirmation check failed in 'Perform Maintenance Jobs' app

 

Inspection Lot Usage Decision: New Follow-Up Actions

Figure 18: Phase Model for the Maintenance Process

With the new Follow-Up Actions you can enable the system to automatically change the system status of a technical object during checklist completion. For this purpose, three new function modules are available that you can specify as follow-up actions for usage decisions in Customizing. The function modules either set or reset the new technical object system status FLCO (Faulty Condition).

The following new function modules are available in function group EAM_CL_FOL:

  • EAM_CL_SET_TOBJ_STATUS_FLCO (Checklists Follow-Up Action: Set FLCO status on technical object)
    This follow-up action can be used to set the new technical object status FLCO independently of the inspection lot usage decision valuation:
    - The FLCO status becomes active, if it is not active.
    - You can use this follow-up action for example to accept an inspection lot based on visual characteristics, but the technical object has a technical faulty condition.
  • EAM_CL_RES_TOBJ_STATUS_FLCO (Checklists Follow-Up Action: Reset FLCO status on technical object)
    This follow-up action can be used to reset the new technical object status FLCO independently of the inspection lot usage decision valuation:
    - The FLCO status becomes inactive, if it is not inactive.
    - You can use this follow-up action for example to reject an inspection lot based on visual characteristics, but the technical condition is still valid.
  • EAM_CL_CHG_TOBJ_STATUS_FLCO (Checklists Follow-Up Action: Set or reset FLCO on tech obj based on UD) 
    This follow-up action can be used to dynamically set or remove the system status FLCO for the technical object of an inspection lot based on a predefined logic that considers the inspection lot valuation: A Rejected usage decision sets the status FLCO on the technical object. An Accepted usage decision removes the status FLCO from the technical object.

Note that the system status FLCO is informational in nature and does not trigger any business transactions on its own. However, you can control the execution of specific business transactions as a consequence of this status by defining custom user statuses that react to the setting and resetting of this status.

Integrated checklist-handling in Find Technical Objects and Find Maintenance Orders and Operations

Find Technical Objects app (F2072)

The Find Technical Objects app offers now the new filter group Checklists that enables you to find technical objects for which inspection checklists have been created and to view the open checklists for a technical object. 
The new Has Checklist column in the results table highlights the inspection relevance.
The new Open Checklists column in the results table shows the number of inspection lots for which no usage decision has been made. By clicking on the number, you can view the corresponding inspection lots and open each of them in the Record Inspection Results app (F1685A) or you can navigate to the Manage Inspection Checklists app (F6607) by clicking on the feeter line of the Open Checklists popover. If no open checklists exist, the system navigates to the Manage Inspection Checklists app (F6607) and displays a prefiltered list of all closed inspection lots with reference to that technical object. This cross-app navigation requires authorization for the apps F1685A and F6607.

New SAP Fiori app Manage Inspection Checklists

The new SAP Fiori app Manage Inspection Checklists (F6607) allows you to view and manage inspection checklists that have been created with reference to maintenance order operations, technical objects, and inspection lots.

You can display and process the results list on the level of individual inspection checklists (inspection lots) and inspection characteristics.
The results list provides detailed information about the relevant inspection lots and inspection characteristics, including the results recording status and usage decision valuation. In addition, it enables you to perform the following actions:

  • Record usage decisions
  • Copy or replace inspection lots
  • Activate or deactivate inspection lots
  • Navigate from an inspection lot to the Display Inspection Lot app (F2180)
  • Navigate from an inspection characteristic to the Record Inspection Results app (F1685A)
  • Navigate to other related apps by means of smart links in the results list

The screenshot below highlights the Adapt Filter button that opens a pop-up window with dedicated filter groups for the associated objects with value helps for the filters (see red dashed line) and the links for semantic object based cross-app navigation (see yellow dashed line).

Figure 26: New SAP Fiori app 'Manage Inspection Checklists'

Inspection lots are displayed with information from the corresponding maintenance order. You can filter by maintenance order and inspection lot, as well as by other criteria. The next screenshot shows the filter based on usage decision valuation:

Figure 27: 'Manage Inspection Checklists' app - Filter based on usage decision valuation

The Inspection Lot Details popover displays inspection lot operation (see red dashed box) above the inspection characteristics with their valuation status:

Figure 28: 'Manage Inspection Checklists' app - Inspection Lot Details

Actions can be executed on the inspection lots:

  • Set Usage Decision
  • Activate
  • Deactivate
  • Copy
  • Replace

Closed Loop

Integrated, cross-app navigation from the Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders app and Find Maintenance Orders app through BAdI implementation

The new Business Add-In (BAdI) Cross-App Navigation Control for Fiori Applications (EAM_CROSS_APP_NAV_CONTROL) allows you to display custom cross-app navigation links in the following SAP Fiori apps:

  • Find Maintenance Orders (F2175)
  • Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders (F4604)

The custom cross-app navigation allows you to open selected items in other Fiori apps, including apps that are
not part of SAP S/4HANA.

When you create an implementation, the additional quick action Open In becomes available in the table toolbar of the app. Business users can use this quick action to display a dropdown menu with links to the target applications that you have defined in the implementation.
In your implementation, you can define relevant target applications, link texts, and system behavior of the
cross-app navigation according to your requirements. If a target application is not included in the business
catalog assignment of a user, the respective link will not be visible.

The BAdI is available for Key User Extensibility in the Custom Logic app for for business context:

  • Asset Management: Maintenance Order
  • Maintenance Notification

The BAdI is filter enabled and is initial working for Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders (F4604).

The screenshot below shows the creation of a new implementation of BAdI EAM_CROSS_APP_NAV_CONTROL with the 

  1. Business Context
  2. Dedicated BAdI
  3. BAdI Filter

Figure 30: 'Custom Logic' app - BAdI EAM_CROSS_APP_NAV_CONTROL for cross-app navigations

As an example, the next figure shows the usage of the implemented BAdI. See the following explanations of the numbers in the figure:

  1. Open In will be available, if an active BAdI-implementation is available and at least one authorization* for a cross-app navigation. * Authorization means an assigned PFCG role with a Fiori business catalog, that contains the target mapping (intent-based navigation)
  2. Translated individualized cross-app navigation labels possible by using Custom Code list from F3248
  3. Cross-app navigation label will be derived from the target mapping title
  4. Individualized cross-app navigation label is possible by using the optional actionName parameter
  5. If end users do not have the authorization* for a cross-app navigation, then this is not available. Device types of traget mappings are also considered for cross-app navigation list entries (supported by standard sapui5 function isNavigationSupported)

Figure 31: Cross-app navigation from the Manage Maintenance Notifications and Orders app through BAdI implementation

Cross-application navigation from maintenance-order processing to permit to work 

Now, you can easily create a new work permit or view assigned work permits while processing a maintenance order on the SAP Web User Interface for Plant Maintenance.
When you open a maintenance order in the Change Maintenance Order app or the Display Maintenance Order app, a new navigation option is available in the Additional Functions menu. This option allows you to open the Manage Work Permits app in a new window and perform the following tasks:

  • Create a new work permit with reference to the maintenance order. The planning plant and the order
    number are copied from the maintenance order to the work permit creation dialog. If no work permit is
    assigned to the maintenance order, the system opens the work permit creation dialog by default.
  • View and manage the work permits that are associated with the maintenance order.
  • Remove the work permit assignments of the maintenance order.

The next figure show the cross-app navigation in case the order has no Work Permit assigned:

Figure 32: Cross-application navigation from maintenance-order processing to permit to work

  1. Customizing Change View Maintain Permit to Work Profile for Planning Plant (SM30: VPTWPROFILE)
  2. If the Permit to Work Customizing is enabled for the planning plant, then the cross-app navigation is available
  3. If the order has no Work Permit assigned, then the „Create Work Permit Request“ F4691 app is used for the cross-app navigation
  4. Create Work Permit Request app F4691 opens in a new window with the parameter passing of order number and planning plant

The next figure show the cross-app navigation in case the order has or had a Work Permit assigned:

Figure 33: Cross-application navigation from maintenance-order processing to permit to work

  1. Customizing Change View Maintain Permit to Work Profile for Planning Plant (SM30: VPTWPROFILE)
  2. If the Permit to Work Customizing is enabled for the planning plant, then the cross-app navigation is available
  3. If the order has or had a Work Permit assigned (PTW020/ PTW010), then the „Manage Work Permits“ F6579 app is used for the cross-app navigation and opens in a new window
  4. Prepopulate the order number and the planning plant to F6579 app
  5. Creating an additional Work Permit uses as well the parameter passing of order number and planning plant

Note: In Order Create mode (no order number stored on DB site), then the cross-app navigation is available but inactive as shown in the screenshot below:
